past_medical_history: Adenocarcinoma of the paranasal sinuses with skull base infiltration, diagnosed 8 months ago
Microsurgical resection of paranasal sinus tumor via anterior skull base approach 6 months ago
Adjuvant radiochemotherapy (cisplatin/5-FU + 60 Gy fractionated radiation) completed 3 months ago
Chronic sinusitis, managed medically for 10 years prior to tumor diagnosis
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled with borderline HbA1c

medications.indication: Post-surgical and post-radiation cerebral edema management

family_history: Father deceased at age 72 from lung cancer
Mother alive with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
One brother with coronary artery disease, age 63
No known family history of epilepsy or neurological disorders

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Occasional wine with meals, approximately 2-3 glasses per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in single-story home
social_history.functional_status: Was independent in all ADLs prior to current admission; has been fatigued since completing radiochemotherapy

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but confused. Oriented to person only, not to time or place. Attention impaired -- unable to spell WORLD backwards or perform serial 7s. Speech is fluent but with occasional paraphasic errors. Unable to recall 3 objects at 5 minutes. Intermittent periods of vacant staring lasting 10-30 seconds with lip-smacking automatisms, after which patient appears more confused.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN I: Not tested. CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally, fundi normal. CN III/IV/VI: Pupils equal and reactive 3mm to 2mm, extraocular movements full without nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact V1-V3 bilaterally. CN VII: Mild right lower facial asymmetry noted intermittently but not consistently reproducible. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact to finger rub. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal. Tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities, no drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact throughout. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ throughout upper and lower extremities bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. No dysmetria.
neurological_exam.gait: Unsteady, wide-based. Patient requires one-person assist. Tandem gait not attempted due to instability.
neurological_exam.additional: Intermittent episodes of behavioral arrest with oral automatisms (lip-smacking, chewing movements) lasting 10-30 seconds observed during examination. Patient appears amnestic for these episodes. No convulsive activity witnessed. Post-ictally, patient becomes more disoriented and somnolent for several minutes before returning to baseline confused state.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive confusion and episodes of unresponsiveness over the past 10 days
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. R is a 59-year-old right-handed man with a recent history of adenocarcinoma of the paranasal sinuses infiltrating the skull base, treated with microsurgical resection approximately 6 months ago followed by adjuvant radiochemotherapy completed 3 months ago. He was in his usual post-treatment state until approximately 10 days prior to admission when his wife noticed increasing forgetfulness and disorientation. He began misplacing items, asking repetitive questions, and on two occasions became lost within his own home. Over the following days, his confusion worsened progressively. Five days before admission, his wife observed him having episodes of staring blankly for 20-30 seconds, during which he would make chewing or lip-smacking movements and was unresponsive to verbal commands. These episodes occurred 3-4 times daily and were followed by a period of increased drowsiness lasting several minutes. He had no convulsive activity witnessed by his family. Three days before admission, he was found sitting in a chair, essentially unresponsive, and was brought to the emergency department. On arrival he was somnolent but arousable, opening eyes to voice. He could state his name but was disoriented to time, place, and situation. A low-grade fever of 37.9 degrees Celsius was noted. There was concern for infectious or paraneoplastic etiology given his oncologic history. He was admitted to the neurology service for further evaluation. Since admission, the episodic staring spells with oral automatisms have continued, and the patient has had fluctuating levels of consciousness, with periods of relative alertness alternating with obtundation. Nursing staff have documented at least 8-10 such episodes per day. There is no history of prior seizures. No recent travel, sick contacts, or new medication changes. His dexamethasone had been tapered from 8 mg twice daily to 4 mg twice daily two weeks before symptom onset.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Poorly organized, 6-7 Hz theta activity posteriorly, no clear alpha rhythm
background.symmetry: Asymmetric -- right hemisphere shows greater amplitude slowing

findings.morphology: Sharply contoured triphasic-appearing waves with occasional polyspike components
findings.state: Persistent in both wakefulness and drowsiness

findings.location: Bilateral frontotemporal, right greater than left
findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic delta with occasional sharp morphology
findings.state: More prominent during periods of clinical obtundation

findings.location: Right temporal onset (T4, T6) with secondary spread to right frontal and bilateral regions
findings.frequency: Evolving from 2 Hz to 6-8 Hz rhythmic sharp-wave activity
findings.morphology: Recruiting rhythmic sharp waves with clear evolution in frequency, amplitude, and field
findings.state: Multiple seizures captured during 2-hour recording; clinical correlate: behavioral arrest with oral automatisms

artifacts.impact: Intermittently obscures temporal sharp activity during chewing automatisms

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to altered mental status

eeg.impression: Severely abnormal EEG demonstrating right frontotemporal periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges with multiple electrographic seizures arising from the right temporal region. Background diffusely slow and poorly organized, right worse than left, suggesting widespread cerebral dysfunction with right hemispheric predominance. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Muscle artifact during automatisms partially obscures temporal channels. Longer-term continuous monitoring recommended.

findings.location: Anterior skull base, ethmoidal and sphenoidal sinus region
findings.size: Surgical cavity approximately 4.2 x 3.1 x 2.8 cm

signal_characteristics.T1: Mixed signal within surgical cavity, peripheral enhancement
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion within surgical bed

findings.mass_effect: Minimal, stable compared to prior imaging

findings.location: Right mesial temporal lobe including hippocampus, amygdala, and temporal pole; extending to right insular cortex

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense signal with mild gyral swelling
signal_characteristics.DWI: Patchy restricted diffusion in right hippocampus
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Subtle leptomeningeal enhancement over right temporal lobe

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the right temporal horn
findings.borders: Ill-defined, following cortical and subcortical gray matter

findings.location: Left temporal pole and anterior hippocampus

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity, less conspicuous than right side

findings.location: Bilateral frontal white matter adjacent to surgical site

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Patchy hyperintensities consistent with radiation-related changes or gliosis

additional_observations: No evidence of residual or recurrent tumor at the surgical site
Ventricles and sulci appropriate for age
mri.impression: Post-surgical changes at the anterior skull base without evidence of residual or recurrent tumor. Abnormal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity with mild swelling and patchy restricted diffusion involving the right mesial temporal lobe (hippocampus, amygdala, temporal pole) and right insula, with subtle involvement of the left temporal pole. Differential includes viral encephalitis (particularly HSV), autoimmune/limbic encephalitis, post-ictal changes from ongoing seizures, or less likely radiation-induced changes. Post-treatment white matter changes in bilateral frontal regions, likely radiation-related. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 = 38 mm)
Non-specific ST-T wave changes in lateral leads, likely related to LVH
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria and secondary repolarization changes. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 12.4 x10^9/L (H), Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 35.2 % (L), Neutrophils 78 % (H), Lymphocytes 14 % (L), Glucose 168 mg/dL (H), Albumin 3.4 g/dL (L), CRP 18.4 mg/L (H), Prolactin 42 ng/mL (H), HbA1c 6.8 % (H).

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 18 cm. WBC: 3 cells/uL (normal: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL (normal: 15-45). Glucose: 72 mg/dL (normal: 40-70, serum glucose 168) (ratio 0.43 (normal: >0.6)). HSV_1_PCR: POSITIVE. HSV_2_PCR: Negative. VZV_PCR: Negative. Enterovirus_PCR: Negative. Gram_stain: No organisms seen. Cytology: No malignant cells identified. Oligoclonal_bands: Not detected.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: No identifiable posterior dominant rhythm; diffuse theta-delta slowing
background.symmetry: Asymmetric, right hemisphere with higher amplitude periodic discharges
background.continuity: Continuous with periodic and evolving patterns

findings.location: Right frontotemporal (F8, T4) evolving to bilateral (poly)spike-wave complexes
findings.frequency: 1.5-2.5 Hz, with intermittent evolution to 4-6 Hz recruiting rhythms
findings.morphology: Polyspike-wave complexes with superimposed fast activity
findings.state: Persistent across all states of arousal over 48-hour monitoring

findings.location: Right temporal onset with rapid bilateral spread
findings.frequency: 38 seizures captured over 48 hours, average duration 45-90 seconds
findings.morphology: Evolving rhythmic sharp-wave activity with clear onset, evolution, and offset
findings.state: Occur in wakefulness and sleep; no circadian variation

findings.morphology: Diffuse voltage attenuation with slow recovery over 30-60 seconds

output.impression: Periodic lateralized discharges with evolution to bilateral polyspike-wave complexes. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Right mesial temporal lobe -- hippocampus, amygdala, parahippocampal gyrus, and temporal pole. Now with extension to right insular cortex and right inferior frontal gyrus.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Increased hyperintensity and swelling compared to prior study (5 days ago)
signal_characteristics.DWI: More extensive restricted diffusion now involving the right hippocampus and insular ribbon
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: New patchy parenchymal enhancement in right temporal lobe in addition to prior leptomeningeal enhancement
signal_characteristics.MR_spectroscopy: Reduced NAA/Cr ratio in right hippocampus (1.2, normal >1.6) with elevated choline suggesting neuronal loss and inflammation

findings.mass_effect: Mild uncal herniation with effacement of the right ambient cistern

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Mildly increased signal compared to prior, now more conspicuous

signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Stable peripheral enhancement at surgical margin, no new nodular enhancement to suggest recurrence

additional_observations: Stable bilateral frontal white matter changes from radiation
output.impression: Interval progression of right mesial temporal lobe signal abnormality with new parenchymal enhancement, more extensive restricted diffusion, and early uncal herniation. Left temporal pole changes are mildly more prominent. No tumor recurrence. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: ANA 1:80 speckled  (H), ESR 38 mm/hr (H).

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 1500 mg IV BID, Lacosamide 200 mg IV BID, Acyclovir 750 mg IV Q8H
interactions: Levetiracetam + Metformin: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Both renally cleared; monitor renal function.
Acyclovir + Metformin: Both renally eliminated. Acyclovir may reduce renal clearance. Monitor creatinine and adjust doses if renal function declines.
Lacosamide + Amlodipine: Lacosamide prolongs PR interval. Amlodipine may cause mild PR prolongation. Monitor ECG for conduction abnormalities.
warnings: Acyclovir requires adequate hydration to prevent crystalline nephropathy. Ensure IV fluid administration.
Dexamethasone may reduce seizure threshold and exacerbate hyperglycemia. Consider taper as clinical condition allows, but risk of rebound cerebral edema in this patient with HSV encephalitis.
Levetiracetam may cause behavioral side effects (agitation, irritability) -- difficult to distinguish from encephalitis symptoms in this patient.
output.formulary_status: All proposed medications are on formulary
alternatives: If levetiracetam causes behavioral toxicity, consider brivaracetam as an alternative
If lacosamide causes PR prolongation, consider perampanel or clobazam

output.query: HSV encephalitis refractory status epilepticus management

results.title: Super-refractory status epilepticus in HSV encephalitis: outcomes and treatment strategies
results.summary: Retrospective analysis of 34 patients with HSV encephalitis complicated by super-refractory status epilepticus. Median duration of SE was 21 days. Burst-suppression with midazolam/propofol required in 76%. Ketamine added in 41%. Immunotherapy (steroids, IVIG, plasma exchange) attempted in 62% with variable response. Overall mortality was 29%, with significant morbidity among survivors. Early aggressive antiviral and antiseizure therapy associated with better outcomes.

results.title: The role of autoimmunity in post-HSV encephalitis epilepsy
results.summary: Evidence suggests that HSV encephalitis can trigger secondary autoimmune encephalitis, particularly NMDA receptor antibody-mediated. Patients with refractory seizures post-HSV should be tested for NMDA receptor antibodies in both serum and CSF, even weeks after initial presentation. Immunotherapy may be beneficial in this subset.

results.title: Electroencephalographic patterns in herpes simplex encephalitis
results.summary: Periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) are the hallmark EEG finding in HSV encephalitis, present in 65-80% of cases. PLEDs in the temporal regions have high sensitivity but moderate specificity for HSE. Nonconvulsive status epilepticus complicates 30-40% of HSE cases and is associated with worse outcomes.

output.summary: HSV encephalitis is a well-recognized cause of super-refractory status epilepticus. PLEDs on EEG are characteristic. Management requires high-dose IV acyclovir (10 mg/kg Q8H for minimum 14-21 days), aggressive antiseizure therapy, and consideration of immunotherapy for suspected post-infectious autoimmune component. Secondary NMDA receptor antibody encephalitis is an important delayed complication to monitor for.

findings.region: Right mesial temporal lobe, hippocampus, amygdala, and temporal pole
findings.signal: FDG_uptake: Markedly increased (SUVmax 12.4, contralateral temporal SUVmax 5.8); pattern: Focal intense uptake with surrounding mild hypermetabolism
FDG_uptake: Mildly increased compared to uninvolved cortex (SUVmax 7.2)

findings.region: Bilateral frontal lobes, right greater than left
findings.signal: FDG_uptake: Reduced, consistent with post-radiation changes and/or diaschisis

output.impression: Marked FDG hypermetabolism in right mesial temporal structures with mild left temporal involvement. Frontal hypometabolism likely post-radiation effect. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe, secondary to HSV-1 encephalitis, presenting as nonconvulsive status epilepticus in a post-neurosurgical/radiochemotherapy patient

differential.diagnosis: HSV encephalitis with secondary temporal lobe epilepsy and status epilepticus
differential.key_distinguishing: Positive HSV-1 PCR in CSF, bilateral temporal MRI changes with right predominance, PLEDs on EEG, progressive course
Oncologic history raises concern, but comprehensive antibody panel is negative. Mesial temporal signal abnormality overlaps with HSE. Would expect subacute onset over weeks-months rather than days.
Recent cancer history, but MRI shows no recurrent tumor. CSF cytology negative. Temporal lobe changes are outside the prior tumor location.
Recent radiotherapy, but temporal lobe involvement is outside typical radiation field for anterior skull base tumors. Timeline (3 months post-radiation) is early for delayed radiation necrosis.

differential.diagnosis: Post-ictal changes from cryptogenic status epilepticus
differential.key_distinguishing: Seizures could cause reversible MRI signal changes, but positive HSV PCR and progressive imaging changes argue against this being purely seizure-related

optimal_actions.action: Obtain urgent EEG to evaluate episodic altered awareness and fluctuating consciousness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right frontotemporal periodic discharges and electrographic seizures consistent with NCSE

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to evaluate temporal lobe pathology and exclude structural lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral temporal lobe signal abnormality, right greater than left, with restricted diffusion

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including viral PCR panel
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive HSV-1 PCR despite bland inflammatory profile due to immunosuppression

optimal_actions.action: Initiate empiric IV acyclovir immediately based on clinical suspicion of HSV encephalitis
Load IV antiseizure medications (levetiracetam, lacosamide) for NCSE
Obtain baseline labs including CBC, BMP, hepatic panel, CRP, and prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild leukocytosis, lymphopenia, elevated CRP, elevated prolactin

optimal_actions.action: Send paraneoplastic and autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative antibody panel, reducing likelihood of autoimmune etiology

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous EEG monitoring for seizure burden assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: High seizure burden confirming refractory status epilepticus

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions between acyclovir, AEDs, and current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acyclovir-metformin renal interaction, lacosamide PR prolongation risk

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on HSV encephalitis and refractory status epilepticus management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence for aggressive antiviral therapy and consideration of immunotherapy for post-HSV autoimmune component

critical_actions: Recognize nonconvulsive status epilepticus from EEG findings
Order lumbar puncture with HSV PCR given temporal lobe involvement
Initiate empiric IV acyclovir without waiting for PCR results in suspected HSV encephalitis
Initiate aggressive IV antiseizure medication therapy for status epilepticus
Consider immunocompromised state (post-chemotherapy, on dexamethasone) as explanation for bland CSF despite active infection
Do not attribute seizures solely to structural cause from prior tumor without evaluating for infectious etiology
contraindicated_actions: Delaying acyclovir until CSF results return -- HSV encephalitis requires empiric treatment
Performing MRI-guided biopsy of temporal lobe signal abnormality without first ruling out HSV encephalitis
Attributing all findings to post-radiation changes and delaying workup
Administering carbamazepine or phenytoin as sole AED without considering hepatic interactions with acyclovir and existing medications
Rapidly tapering dexamethasone during active HSV encephalitis with cerebral edema
Discharging patient without continuous EEG monitoring to confirm seizure control
key_reasoning_points: Bilateral temporal lobe involvement (right > left) on MRI with restricted diffusion is the hallmark imaging pattern of HSV encephalitis, not typical mesial temporal sclerosis or post-radiation change
Positive HSV-1 CSF PCR with normal cell count and protein is atypical but occurs in immunocompromised patients -- dexamethasone and recent chemotherapy suppress the expected inflammatory CSF response
PLEDs on EEG in the temporal region have high sensitivity for HSV encephalitis and should trigger immediate viral PCR testing
The oncologic history (paranasal sinus adenocarcinoma) creates a strong red herring toward paraneoplastic or structural causes, requiring the clinician to also consider common infectious etiologies
Structural cause from the prior tumor/surgery involves the anterior skull base, NOT the temporal lobes -- anatomic discordance should prompt search for an independent temporal lobe pathology
The immunosuppressed state from radiochemotherapy and corticosteroids increases susceptibility to HSV reactivation and modifies the expected CSF findings
Status epilepticus in HSV encephalitis can become super-refractory and may have both infectious and secondary autoimmune components

metadata.difficulty_description: Complex presentation in a post-neurosurgical oncology patient where the recent cancer history, skull base surgery, and radiochemotherapy create strong red herrings toward paraneoplastic or structural etiologies. The actual diagnosis (HSV encephalitis causing temporal lobe seizures and NCSE) requires recognizing the characteristic bilateral temporal MRI pattern, obtaining CSF for viral PCR, and interpreting a bland CSF profile in the context of immunosuppression. Multiple competing diagnoses and atypical CSF findings make this a diagnostic puzzle.
